map.condition: Build prefix-free code minimizing expected length

map.use: \text{Sort by finish time; pick if } s_i \ge f_{\text{prev}}

map.condition: Schedule jobs with deadlines to maximize profit
map.use: \text{Sort by profit desc; place in latest free slot } \le d_i

map.condition: Merge files/runs minimizing total record movements
map.use: \text{Optimal Merge: use min-heap, cost} = \sum \text{(internal node weights)}

map.condition: Find shortest paths from a single source

formulas.name: Greedy Choice Property
formulas.latex: \text{A locally optimal (greedy) choice leads to a globally optimal solution}
formulas.plain: A locally optimal (greedy) choice leads to a globally optimal solution
formulas.whenToUse: Verify before applying greedy — must hold for the problem to admit a greedy solution

terms.means: best choice at the current step without reconsidering past choices

formulas.trap: Not every optimization problem has the greedy-choice property; confusing it with DP leads to wrong answers.

formulas.name: Optimal Substructure
formulas.latex: \text{Optimal solution contains optimal solutions to its subproblems}
formulas.plain: Optimal solution contains optimal solutions to its subproblems
formulas.whenToUse: Required condition (along with greedy-choice property) for a greedy algorithm to be correct

terms.means: smaller instance of the same problem after a greedy decision

formulas.trap: Optimal substructure alone is insufficient — DP also has it; greedy additionally needs the greedy-choice property.

formulas.name: Activity Selection — Greedy Rule
formulas.latex: \text{Sort activities by finish time } f_i; \text{ select } a_i \text{ if } s_i \ge f_{\text{last selected}}
formulas.plain: Sort activities by finish time; select activity i if its start time >= finish time of last selected activity
formulas.whenToUse: Maximizing the number of non-overlapping activities (intervals) from a given set

terms.means: start time of activity i
finish time of activity i
finish time of the most recently selected activity

formulas.trap: Sorting by start time or duration does NOT yield the optimal solution; must sort by finish time.

formulas.name: Activity Selection — Time Complexity
formulas.plain: T(n) = O(n log n) — sorting dominates
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing time needed to solve activity selection on n activities

terms.means: number of activities

formulas.trap: After sorting, the greedy scan is O(n); forgetting to count the sort gives incorrect O(n).

formulas.name: Job Scheduling — Greedy Rule
formulas.latex: \text{Sort jobs by profit } P_i \downarrow; \text{ place job in latest free slot } t \le d_i
formulas.plain: Sort jobs by profit descending; place each job in the latest available slot <= its deadline
formulas.whenToUse: Selecting a subset of jobs with deadlines to maximize total profit (each job takes 1 unit)

terms.means: profit of job i if completed on time
deadline of job i (must finish by slot d_i)

formulas.trap: Placing a job in the earliest free slot (instead of latest) may block later jobs with the same deadline.

formulas.name: Job Scheduling — Feasibility Condition
formulas.latex: \text{A set } S \text{ of jobs is feasible iff } |S_t| \le t \text{ for all } t = 1,\ldots,n
formulas.plain: A set S of jobs is feasible iff for every t, the number of jobs in S with deadline <= t is at most t
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a selected subset of jobs can all be completed within their deadlines

terms.means: jobs in S with deadline <= t
time slot index

formulas.trap: Ignoring feasibility and simply picking top-k profit jobs without slot allocation gives wrong answers.

formulas.name: Optimal Merge — Total Cost
formulas.plain: Total record movements = sum of weights of all internal nodes in the merge tree
formulas.whenToUse: Finding minimum record movements to merge n sorted files using a 2-way merge

terms.means: weight (total records) at an internal node = sum of its children's sizes

formulas.trap: Counting only leaf-to-root paths or confusing with the number of merges; always sum ALL internal node weights.

formulas.name: Optimal Merge — Min-Heap Algorithm
formulas.latex: \text{Repeatedly extract two smallest files, merge them (cost = sum), insert result back}
formulas.plain: Use a min-heap: repeatedly pick two smallest files, merge (cost = their total size), reinsert merged file
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the minimum cost merge order for n files (same structure as Huffman coding)

terms.means: priority queue enabling O(log n) extraction of minimum

formulas.trap: This is identical to Huffman coding; the 'cost' here is total record movements, not code length.

formulas.name: Huffman — Expected Code Length
formulas.plain: L = sum over all symbols of (probability_i * code_length_i)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the expected (average) number of bits per symbol for a given Huffman encoding

terms.means: probability (or relative frequency) of symbol i
bit-length of the Huffman code assigned to symbol i
number of symbols in the alphabet

formulas.trap: Using frequency counts instead of probabilities changes the raw sum but NOT the relative optimality; normalize if needed.

formulas.name: Huffman — Building the Tree
formulas.latex: \text{Min-heap: extract two nodes with smallest freq, combine (freq}_{\text{new}} = f_1 + f_2\text{), insert}
formulas.plain: Min-heap: extract two lowest-frequency nodes, create parent with frequency = f1 + f2, reinsert; repeat until one node
formulas.whenToUse: Constructing an optimal prefix-free code for a given frequency/probability distribution

terms.means: frequencies of the two nodes being merged
frequency of the new combined (internal) node

formulas.trap: When two nodes have equal frequency, the tie-breaking order can produce different but equally optimal trees — all are valid.

formulas.name: Huffman — Time Complexity
formulas.plain: T(n) = O(n log n) — n-1 merge steps each taking O(log n) for heap operations
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing the running time of Huffman coding on an alphabet of size n

terms.means: number of distinct symbols (alphabet size)

formulas.trap: There are exactly n-1 merge steps; forgetting the -1 is a minor error, but more critically confusing n with total text length.

formulas.name: Huffman — Prefix-Free Property
formulas.latex: \text{No codeword is a prefix of another codeword (full binary tree leaves = symbols)}
formulas.plain: No codeword is a prefix of another; symbols are leaves of the Huffman tree
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying or explaining why Huffman codes are uniquely decodable without separators

terms.means: property ensuring unambiguous decoding without delimiters

formulas.trap: Confusing prefix-free with fixed-length; Huffman is variable-length but prefix-free.

formulas.name: Huffman — Optimality Result
formulas.latex: H(X) \le L_{\text{Huffman}} < H(X) + 1 \quad \text{where } H(X) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i
formulas.plain: Entropy H(X) <= Huffman expected length < H(X) + 1 (in bits per symbol)
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding the efficiency of Huffman coding relative to the entropy of the source

terms.means: Shannon entropy of the source (theoretical lower bound for lossless coding)
expected code length of the Huffman code

formulas.trap: Huffman achieves entropy only for dyadic distributions (all p_i = 2^{-k}); otherwise there is a gap up to 1 bit.

formulas.name: Huffman — Code Length Bounds
formulas.whenToUse: Estimating the code length assigned to a particular symbol with probability p_i

terms.means: probability of symbol i
Huffman code length for symbol i

formulas.trap: Higher probability symbols get shorter codes; the most frequent symbol may get code length 1 only if it dominates.

formulas.name: Dijkstra — Greedy Relaxation Rule
formulas.latex: d[v] = \min\bigl(d[v],\; d[u] + w(u,v)\bigr) \text{ for each neighbor } v \text{ of extracted } u
formulas.plain: d[v] = min(d[v], d[u] + w(u,v)) — relax edge (u,v) after extracting u with minimum distance
formulas.whenToUse: Finding single-source shortest paths in a graph with non-negative edge weights

terms.means: current known shortest distance from source to v
weight of edge from u to v

formulas.trap: Dijkstra FAILS with negative edge weights — use Bellman-Ford instead.

formulas.name: Dijkstra — Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T = O((V + E) \log V) \text{ with binary heap}
formulas.plain: T = O((V + E) log V) using a binary min-heap (or O(V^2) with adjacency matrix)
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing Dijkstra's algorithm complexity on V vertices and E edges

terms.means: number of vertices
number of edges

formulas.trap: With a Fibonacci heap the complexity improves to O(E + V log V), but binary heap is the GATE-standard assumption.

formulas.name: MST — Prim's & Kruskal's
formulas.latex: \text{Prim: } O(E \log V); \quad \text{Kruskal: } O(E \log E) \approx O(E \log V)
formulas.plain: Prim: O(E log V) with binary heap; Kruskal: O(E log E) ~ O(E log V) using union-find
formulas.whenToUse: Finding a Minimum Spanning Tree of an undirected weighted graph

terms.means: number of edges
number of vertices

formulas.trap: Kruskal's uses Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) to detect cycles; forgetting this makes cycle detection O(V) per edge, ruining complexity.
